Welcome to my CFD project portfolio focused on:

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: During the development of the species transport solver, several numerical and implementation challenges were encountered. Addressing these issues was essential to obtaining a stable and physically meaningful solution.

docs.title: Problem
docs.text: The scalar field exhibited stripe-like oscillations instead of a smooth distribution.

docs.title: Root Cause
docs.text:
• Use of an inappropriate advection treatment
  • Numerical dispersion introduced by the scheme

docs.title: Resolution
docs.text:
• Replaced the initial approach with Basilisk’s advection() operator
  • Ensured proper flux-based transport

docs.title: Key Insight
docs.text: The choice of numerical scheme has a direct impact on solution stability and can introduce non-physical artifacts if not handled correctly.

docs.title: Problem
docs.text: The scalar field moved in an unintended direction (vertical instead of horizontal).

docs.title: Root Cause
docs.text:
• Incorrect assignment of face-centered velocity (uf)
  • Misinterpretation of staggered grid variables

docs.title: Resolution
docs.text:
Assigned velocity components explicitly:

foreach_face(x)
  uf.x[] = 0.2;

foreach_face(y)
  uf.y[] = 0.0;

docs.title: Key Insight
docs.text: Face-centered variables must be handled carefully, especially in staggered grid formulations.

docs.title: 3. Compilation Error Due to Variable Redefinition
Problem
docs.text: Compilation failed due to redefinition of the velocity field (uf).

docs.title: Root Cause
docs.text: • Manual declaration of uf while it was already defined in advection.h

docs.title: Resolution
docs.text:
• Removed redundant declaration
  • Relied on Basilisk’s internal definitions

docs.title: Key Insight
docs.text: Understanding the internal structure of the solver framework helps avoid conflicts and redundant definitions.

docs.title: Problem
docs.text: Compilation error indicating an undefined reference to tracers.

docs.title: Root Cause
docs.text: • Basilisk requires a tracer list for scalar transport

docs.title: Resolution
docs.text:
Defined tracer explicitly:

scalar * tracers = {C};

docs.title: Key Insight
docs.text: Framework-specific requirements must be satisfied even if they are not immediately obvious.

docs.title: Problem
docs.text: The scalar field (blob) was not visible in the output visualization.

docs.title: Root Cause
docs.text:
• Improper visualization scaling
  • Very low magnitude values of concentration

docs.title: Resolution
docs.text:
• Adjusted visualization range (min and max)
  • Tuned initial condition for better visibility

docs.title: Key Insight
docs.text: Visualization parameters can significantly affect interpretation and may hide correct physical behavior.

docs.title: Problem
docs.text: The scalar field dissipated too quickly, losing its structure.

docs.title: Root Cause
docs.text:
• High diffusion coefficient
  • Strong reaction rate

docs.title: Resolution
docs.text:
• Reduced diffusion coefficient
  • Adjusted reaction rate constant

docs.title: Key Insight
docs.text: Physical parameters must be carefully balanced to capture meaningful system behavior.

docs.title: Problem
docs.text: The simulation became unstable or produced inaccurate results.

docs.title: Root Cause
docs.text: • Time step too large for explicit time integration

docs.title: Resolution
docs.text:
Reduced time step:

DT = 1e-4;

docs.title: Key Insight
docs.text: Time step size is critical for stability, especially in explicit schemes.

docs.title: Summary
docs.text:
These challenges highlight that CFD development involves more than solving equations. It requires:

  • Careful selection of numerical methods
  • Understanding of solver internals
  • Systematic debugging of both numerical and implementation issues

Resolving these problems significantly improved both the accuracy of the solution and the understanding of computational fluid dynamics principles.

docs.title: Model Extensions
docs.text:
• Extend to multi-species transport
  • Include interaction between species
  • Implement realistic reaction mechanisms

docs.title: Physics Enhancements
docs.text:
• Couple with Navier–Stokes equations
  • Add temperature equation (energy transport)
  • Simulate combustion-like problems

docs.title: Numerical Improvements
docs.text:
• Implement adaptive mesh refinement
  • Explore higher-order advection schemes
  • Improve time integration methods

docs.title: Application-Oriented Studies
docs.text:
• Pollutant dispersion simulations
  • HVAC airflow and contaminant transport
  • Heat transfer in fluid systems

docs.title: Summary
docs.text: Future extensions of this work will move from simplified scalar transport toward fully coupled multiphysics simulations used in real-world engineering problems.

docs.title: What Equation is Being Solved?
docs.text:
This project solves a species transport equation, which describes how a substance (species) moves and changes inside a flowing medium.

The governing equation is:

∂C/∂t + ∇·(uC) = D∇²C − kC

Where:

  • C → concentration of the species
  • u → velocity field (flow direction and speed)
  • D → diffusion coefficient
  • k → reaction rate

docs.title: Physical Meaning of Each Term
docs.text:
• ∂C/∂t Change of concentration with time

  • ∇·(uC) Transport due to fluid motion (Advection)

  • D∇²C Spreading due to concentration gradients (Diffusion)

  • −kC Decay or consumption of species (Reaction)

docs.title: How This Relates to Real CFD Problems
docs.text:
In real-world simulations, this equation is used to model:

  • Pollutant transport in air and water
  • Mixing of different fluids
  • Heat transfer (temperature behaves like a scalar)
  • Combustion and chemical reactions

docs.title: Important Note
docs.text:
This project solves a single-species transport problem, which is a simplified version of real industrial simulations.

In advanced cases, multiple species interact with each other through complex reactions.

docs.title: Implementation
docs.text:
The solver is implemented using Basilisk C.

Key steps:

  • Initialize scalar field (Gaussian blob)
  • Define velocity field
  • Solve advection using Basilisk's advection module
  • Solve diffusion using diffusion operator
  • Apply reaction term

docs.title: 1. Numerical Schemes Matter
docs.text: Using a centered scheme for advection caused non-physical oscillations (stripes). This highlighted the importance of selecting stable numerical methods.

docs.title: 2. Difference Between Physical and Numerical Diffusion
docs.text:
• Physical diffusion: controlled by diffusivity (D)
  • Numerical diffusion: introduced by discretization

Understanding this distinction was critical to interpreting results.

docs.title: 3. Importance of Scaling in Visualization
docs.text: Incorrect scaling initially made the blob invisible. Proper min/max values are essential for meaningful visualization.

docs.title: 4. Mesh Resolution Effects
docs.text:
Increasing grid resolution (128 → 512):

  • Improved solution accuracy
  • Reduced numerical errors
  • Better captured gradients

docs.title: 5. Coupled Physics Understanding
docs.text:
This project demonstrated how:

  • Advection transports the scalar
  • Diffusion spreads it
  • Reaction reduces it

All three processes interact simultaneously.

docs.title: 6. Debugging CFD Simulations
docs.text:
Several issues were encountered and resolved:

  • Compilation errors due to missing includes
  • Instability due to large time steps
  • Incorrect field definitions (uf, Df)
  • Visualization issues

This significantly improved debugging and solver-level understanding.

docs.title: Model Simplifications
docs.text:
• Single-species transport model
  • No interaction between multiple species
  • Reaction modeled as simple first-order decay

docs.title: Flow Assumptions
docs.text:
• Constant velocity field
  • No coupling with Navier–Stokes equations
  • No turbulence effects

docs.title: Numerical Limitations
docs.text:
• Explicit time stepping requires small time steps
  • Grid resolution impacts accuracy
  • No adaptive mesh refinement used in this setup

docs.title: Physical Simplifications
docs.text:
• No temperature coupling (no energy equation)
  • Constant diffusion coefficient
  • No real chemical kinetics

docs.title: Summary
docs.text: This project focuses on understanding the fundamentals of scalar transport. While simplified, it provides a strong foundation for more complex CFD simulations.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
This section explains how the governing species transport equation is solved numerically.

The equation:

∂C/∂t + ∇·(uC) = D∇²C − kC

is solved using a finite-volume approach with time-stepping.

docs.title: Finite Volume Method (FVM)
docs.text:
The computational domain is divided into small control volumes (cells).

Why this method? - Ensures conservation of mass and species - Widely used in industrial CFD solvers

Concept: Each cell stores an average value of concentration (C), and fluxes across cell faces determine how C changes.

docs.title: Explicit Time Stepping
docs.text:
The solution is advanced in time using small time steps (Δt).

Why used here? - Simple to implement - Easy to understand for transient problems

Limitation: - Requires small time step for stability

docs.title: Equation Term:
docs.text: ∇·(uC)

docs.title: Numerical Treatment:
docs.text:
• Solved using Basilisk’s built-in advection() function
  • Uses a stable flux-based scheme

Why this is important: Initially, a naive or centered approach caused: - Non-physical oscillations - Stripe-like artifacts

Switching to a proper advection scheme: - Eliminated oscillations - Ensured stable transport

docs.title: Equation Term:
docs.text: D∇²C

docs.title: Numerical Treatment:
docs.text:
• Solved using Basilisk’s diffusion() function
  • Uses an implicit multigrid solver

Why implicit? - Stable even for larger time steps - Efficient for solving diffusion equations

docs.title: Equation Term:
docs.text: −kC

docs.title: Numerical Treatment:
docs.text: ```text C_new = C / (1 + k·dt)

docs.title: Objective
docs.text:
To develop a 2D finite-volume CFD solver for scalar transport including advection, diffusion, and reaction using Basilisk.

This project implements a simplified species transport solver, commonly used in CFD to simulate transport and reaction of scalar quantities.

docs.title: Problem Description
docs.text:
A scalar concentration field (C) is initialized as a Gaussian blob and transported in a uniform velocity field.

The governing equation solved is:

∂C/∂t + ∇·(uC) = D∇²C − kC

The scalar undergoes:

  • Advection (transport by flow)
  • Diffusion (spreading due to gradients)
  • Reaction (decay over time)

docs.title: In A NUTSHELL:--
docs.text:
Imagine you drop a drop of ink into flowing water.

Three things will happen:

  1. The ink will move with the water → this is Advection
  2. The ink will spread out and mix → this is Diffusion
  3. The ink will fade or disappear over time → this is Reaction

This project simulates exactly this process using mathematics and numerical methods.

docs.title: What is the Scalar here?
docs.text:
A scalar is simply a quantity that has a value at every point in space.

In this project:

  • C represents concentration (like ink density)
  • Each point in the domain has a value of C

docs.title: What is the Computational Domain here?
docs.text:
We simulate the problem inside a square region:

  • This is divided into many small cells (grid)
  • Each cell stores a value of concentration

docs.title: What is the Gaussian Blob here?
docs.text:
The initial condition is a "blob" of concentration.

Mathematically:

C = exp(-100r²)

This creates:

  • High concentration at center
  • Smooth decrease outward

👉 This gives a nice visible shape to track

docs.title: What Does the Solver Do?
docs.text:
At every time step:

  1. Moves the scalar → Advection
  2. Spreads it → Diffusion
  3. Reduces it → Reaction

This process repeats until the final time.

docs.title: Why is This Important?
docs.text:
This type of simulation is used in:

  • Pollution spreading in air/water
  • Heat transfer problems
  • Combustion modeling
  • HVAC airflow analysis

docs.title: Simple Summary
docs.text:
• We start with a blob
  • It moves, spreads, and fades
  • The computer calculates this step by step

docs.title: Advection (Transport by Flow)
docs.text: The scalar is transported by the velocity field without changing its shape significantly.

docs.title: Diffusion (Spreading)
docs.text: The scalar spreads from regions of high concentration to low concentration, smoothing the distribution.

docs.title: Reaction (Decay)
docs.text: The scalar concentration decreases over time due to a decay process.

docs.title: Key Insight
docs.text: The scalar field evolution depends strongly on both physical parameters and the numerical scheme used, which affects stability and accuracy.

docs.title: Result Analysis
docs.text: The simulation captures the expected physical behavior of a reacting scalar transport problem.

docs.title: Observed Behavior
docs.text:
• The scalar field moves in the direction of the velocity field (advection)
  • The concentration spreads over time (diffusion)
  • The magnitude of the scalar decreases gradually (reaction)

docs.title: Diffusion Coefficient (D)
docs.text:
• Higher values → faster spreading
  • Lower values → sharper concentration profile

docs.title: Reaction Rate (k)
docs.text:
• Higher values → faster decay of scalar
  • Lower values → longer persistence of the blob

docs.title: Grid Resolution
docs.text:
• Higher resolution (512) → better shape preservation
  • Lower resolution → increased numerical diffusion

docs.title: Time Step (Δt)
docs.text:
• Smaller time step → stable and accurate solution
  • Larger time step → potential instability

docs.title: Validation
docs.text:
The results are consistent with theoretical expectations of the species transport equation:

  • Transport follows flow direction
  • Diffusion smooths gradients
  • Reaction reduces concentration

This confirms the correctness of the numerical implementation.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: This project was developed using a combination of CFD frameworks, programming tools, and visualization utilities. Each tool was selected based on its role in solving the governing equations efficiently and transparently.

docs.title: Basilisk (CFD Library)
docs.text:
Why it was used: - Easy for learning and implementing the libraries. - Provides built-in numerical solvers for: - Advection - Diffusion - Multigrid methods - Allows direct implementation of governing equations - Offers fine control over numerical methods

Role in Project: - Solves the species transport equation - Handles grid generation and time-stepping - Provides efficient numerical operators

docs.title: C Language
docs.text:
Why it was used: - Required for working with Basilisk - Allows low-level control over numerical operations - High computational efficiency (important for CFD)

Role in Project: - Implements solver logic - Defines simulation events (initialization, advection, diffusion, reaction) - Controls simulation flow

docs.title: Finite Volume Method (FVM)
docs.text:
Why it was used: - Conserves physical quantities (mass, species) - Widely used in industrial CFD solvers

Role in Project: - Discretizes the governing equation over control volumes

docs.title: Explicit Time Stepping
docs.text:
Why it was used: - Simple to implement and understand - Suitable for transient simulations

Trade-off: - Requires small time step for stability

docs.title: Multigrid Solver (Diffusion)
docs.text: Why it was used: - Efficient for solving diffusion equations - Reduces computational cost compared to simple solvers

docs.title: Basilisk Output (output_ppm)
docs.text: Why it was used: - Built-in and easy to integrate with solver - Generates frame-by-frame visualization

docs.title: Video Conversion (ppm2mp4)
docs.text: Why it was used: - Converts simulation frames into a continuous video - Helps visualize transient behavior clearly

docs.title: Linux / WSL
docs.text: Why it was used: - Basilisk is best supported in Linux environments - Provides stable compilation and execution setup

docs.title: GCC / qcc Compiler
docs.text: Why it was used: - Required to compile Basilisk-based C programs - Optimized for numerical computations

docs.title: Git & GitHub
docs.text: Why it was used: - Tracks changes in code and documentation - Enables project versioning and sharing

docs.title: MkDocs + Material Theme
docs.text: Why it was used: - Converts project into structured documentation - Helps present work professionally - Easy deployment via GitHub Pages

docs.title: Summary
docs.text:
Each tool in this project was selected based on its ability to support:

  • Accurate numerical simulation
  • Efficient computation
  • Clear visualization
  • Professional documentation

Together, they form a complete workflow similar to real-world CFD development pipelines.

docs.title: Project 2: Reactive Species Transport with Buoyancy (Basilisk)
Overview
docs.text:
In this project, we extend the classical scalar transport problem into a coupled multi-physics system involving:

  • Species transport
  • Heat generation via reaction
  • Buoyancy-driven flow (natural convection)

This transforms a simple diffusion problem into a reactive flow simulation, which is highly relevant in engineering domains such as combustion, fire dynamics, atmospheric plumes, and chemical reactors.

docs.title: What is being solved?
docs.text:
We simulate the evolution of two scalar fields:

  • Species concentration (C)
  • Temperature (T)

These fields are governed by:

docs.title: 1. Advection–Diffusion of Species
docs.text:
The species is transported due to:

  • Fluid motion (advection)
  • Molecular diffusion

Additionally, species is consumed by a reaction.

docs.title: 2. Heat Equation with Source Term
docs.text:
Temperature evolves due to:

  • Diffusion
  • Heat release from reaction

The reaction acts as a source term, increasing temperature locally.

docs.title: 3. Reaction Model (Arrhenius-type)
docs.text:
A temperature-dependent reaction is used:

  • Reaction rate increases exponentially with temperature
  • This creates nonlinear feedback:

Higher temperature → faster reaction → more heat → even higher temperature

This mechanism is responsible for thermal runaway behavior observed in the results.

docs.title: 4. Fluid Flow (Navier–Stokes)
docs.text:
The flow field is governed by the incompressible Navier–Stokes equations.

A buoyancy force is introduced:

  • Hot fluid becomes lighter and rises
  • Cold fluid sinks

This generates natural convection (plume formation).

docs.title: Why is this important?
docs.text:
This problem captures the core physics of reactive flows, which appear in:

  • Combustion systems (engines, burners)
  • Smoke and fire plume modeling
  • Chemical reactors
  • Atmospheric transport phenomena

It demonstrates how multiple physical processes interact:

  • Diffusion smooths gradients
  • Reaction sharpens gradients
  • Buoyancy redistributes energy

Understanding this interplay is critical in real-world CFD applications.

docs.title: Project 1:
docs.text:
• Solved pure species transport

  • Physics involved:

  • Diffusion

  • Advection (optional)

Behavior was smooth and predictable

docs.title: Project 2:
docs.text: We introduce three major complexities:

docs.title: 1. Reaction Coupling
docs.text:
• Species is no longer conserved
  • It is consumed dynamically
  • Temperature becomes a dependent variable

docs.title: 2. Thermal Feedback
docs.text:
• Reaction depends on temperature
  • Temperature depends on reaction

This creates strong nonlinearity

docs.title: 3. Flow Coupling (Buoyancy)
docs.text:
• Temperature affects density (via buoyancy)
  • Density drives flow
  • Flow transports temperature and species

This creates full two-way coupling

docs.title: Summary of progression
docs.text: Feature Project 1 Project 2 Diffusion Yes Yes Advection Yes Yes Reaction No Yes Heat generation No Yes Fluid flow No Yes Buoyancy No Yes Nonlinearity Low High

docs.title: 1. Thermal Runaway
docs.text:
• Rapid increase in temperature due to reaction feedback
  • Controlled by diffusion (kappa)

docs.title: 2. Diffusion vs Reaction Competition
docs.text:
• High diffusion → stable system
  • Low diffusion → sharp gradients and instability

docs.title: 3. Plume Formation
docs.text:
• Heated fluid rises
  • Forms characteristic mushroom-shaped structures

docs.title: 4. Rayleigh Number Scaling
docs.text:
A dimensionless number is used to quantify buoyancy effects:

  • Higher temperature → higher buoyancy → stronger flow
  • Lower diffusivity → higher Rayleigh number

docs.title: Numerical Method
docs.text:
The simulation is implemented using Basilisk CFD framework, which provides:

  • Finite-volume discretization
  • Adaptive grid capability (extendable)
  • Built-in Navier–Stokes solver
  • Efficient diffusion and advection operators

docs.title: Key Parameters Studied
docs.text:
The main parameter varied in this study is:

  • Thermal diffusivity (kappa)

Values tested:

  • 0.01
  • 0.005
  • 0.002

This allows investigation of:

  • Stability vs instability
  • Reaction intensity
  • Buoyancy strength

docs.title: Outcome
docs.text:
This project demonstrates:

  • Coupled multi-physics simulation capability
  • Understanding of nonlinear reactive systems
  • Ability to extract engineering insights from CFD data

It forms a strong foundation for advanced topics such as:

  • Combustion modeling
  • Turbulent reactive flows
  • Multiphase reacting systems

docs.title: Next Steps
docs.text:
Future improvements may include:

  • Adaptive mesh refinement (AMR)
  • More realistic reaction kinetics
  • Turbulence modeling
  • 3D simulations

This project marks the transition from basic CFD to realistic engineering simulations involving strongly coupled physics.

docs.title: Challenges & Debugging
docs.text: During the setup and execution of the Ahmed body simulation, several issues were encountered and resolved.

docs.title: Summary Table
docs.text: # Issue Cause Fix Key Learning 1 PIMPLE undefined Missing PIMPLE dictionary in fvSolution Added PIMPLE block Solver algorithms depend on correct dictionary structure 2 OmegaFinal missing Required by k-omega SST model Added omegaFinal entry Turbulence models need additional solver controls 3 wallDist undefined Wall distance not specified Added meshWave method Wall models depend on geometric distance 4 div(phi,omega) missing Missing discretization scheme Added scheme in fvSchemes Each equation needs explicit discretization 5 meshWave typo Incorrect spelling Corrected to meshWave Runtime selection is sensitive to typos 6 Courant explosion Time step too large Reduced deltaT, enabled adjustTimeStep, limited maxCo Stability depends on Courant number 7 Forces not generated Function object placed incorrectly Moved forces definition to system/functions Function object placement affects execution 8 Frontal area confusion Incorrect understanding of reference area Used Ahmed body dimensions (0.389 × 0.288 m²) Cd depends on correct reference area

docs.title: Key Takeaways
docs.text:
• OpenFOAM requires strict dictionary structure
  • Turbulence models introduce additional dependencies
  • Numerical stability must be controlled through time stepping
  • Small configuration errors can stop the simulation
  • Correct post-processing setup is essential for meaningful results

docs.title: Future Work
docs.text: Although the current simulation successfully captures the overall flow behavior, several improvements can be made to enhance accuracy and reliability.

docs.title: 1. Mesh Refinement
docs.text:
• Increase mesh resolution in wake region
  • Add local refinement near the rear slant
  • Improve boundary layer resolution

👉 Expected Impact: Better prediction of separation and wake structure, leading to more accurate drag values

docs.title: 2. Grid Independence Study
docs.text:
• Run simulations with multiple mesh densities
  • Compare drag coefficient across meshes

👉 Expected Impact: Ensure results are not dependent on mesh size

docs.title: 3. Near-Wall Modeling
docs.text:
• Refine prism layers near walls
  • Maintain appropriate y+ values

👉 Expected Impact: Improved boundary layer prediction and separation accuracy

docs.title: 4. Time Averaging
docs.text:
• Compute time-averaged drag coefficient
  • Analyze fluctuations in drag force

👉 Expected Impact: More realistic representation of transient flow behavior

docs.title: 5. Validation with Literature
docs.text:
• Compare results with published Ahmed body studies
  • Benchmark against experimental data

👉 Expected Impact: Increase confidence in simulation results

docs.title: 6. Improved Domain Size
docs.text:
• Extend domain boundaries if required
  • Reduce blockage effects

👉 Expected Impact: More realistic external flow conditions

docs.title: 7. Advanced Turbulence Modeling
docs.text:
• Explore DES or LES approaches

👉 Expected Impact: Better capture of unsteady wake structures

docs.title: Final Direction
docs.text:
The next step is to transition from:

  • Qualitative understanding → to
  • Quantitative accuracy

by improving mesh quality, validation, and modeling fidelity.

docs.title: Key Learnings
docs.text: This project provided significant insights into both CFD fundamentals and practical solver usage in OpenFOAM.

docs.title: 1. Solver Understanding (PIMPLE Algorithm)
docs.text:
• Learned how pressure-velocity coupling works in transient simulations
  • Understood the role of outer (PIMPLE) and inner (PISO/SIMPLE) loops
  • Realized that incorrect solver settings can lead to instability or divergence

👉 Key Insight: Solver configuration is not just syntax — it directly affects physical accuracy and stability

docs.title: 2. Turbulence Modeling (k-omega SST)
docs.text:
• Understood why k-omega SST is preferred for external aerodynamics
  • Learned that turbulence models require additional variables (k, omega)
  • Observed sensitivity of results to turbulence modeling

👉 Key Insight: Turbulence models are critical for predicting separation and wake behavior

docs.title: 3. Importance of Wall Distance (wallDist)
docs.text:
• Learned that wall distance is required for near-wall turbulence treatment
  • Understood how wall modeling affects boundary layer prediction

👉 Key Insight: Geometry-related quantities (like wall distance) are essential for turbulence closure

docs.title: 4. Numerical Stability (Courant Number)
docs.text:
• Observed simulation failure due to high Courant number
  • Learned to control time step using:
  • deltaT
  • adjustTimeStep
  • maxCo

👉 Key Insight: Numerical stability must be actively controlled in transient simulations

docs.title: 5. Discretization Schemes Matter
docs.text:
• Learned that missing schemes (e.g., div(phi,omega)) can crash the solver
  • Understood that different schemes affect numerical diffusion and accuracy

👉 Key Insight: Discretization is not just configuration — it influences solution quality

docs.title: 6. Function Objects and Post-Processing
docs.text:
• Learned how OpenFOAM computes forces using function objects
  • Understood the importance of correct placement (system/functions)

👉 Key Insight: Post-processing must be set up correctly during simulation, not after

docs.title: 7. Transient vs Steady Behavior
docs.text:
• Observed that flow remained transient even after 5 seconds
  • Learned concept of statistically steady state

👉 Key Insight: Unsteady flows cannot always be solved using steady-state assumptions

docs.title: 8. Mesh Quality and Accuracy
docs.text:
• Realized that coarse mesh leads to inaccurate drag prediction
  • Observed strong sensitivity of results to mesh resolution

👉 Key Insight: Mesh quality is one of the most critical factors in CFD accuracy

docs.title: 9. Drag Coefficient Sensitivity
docs.text:
• Learned that Cd depends on:
  • Reference area
  • Flow velocity
  • Mesh resolution
  • Wake prediction

👉 Key Insight: Even small mistakes in reference parameters can lead to large errors

docs.title: 10. Debugging Skills in OpenFOAM
docs.text:
• Learned to interpret error messages
  • Fixed issues related to:
  • solver dictionaries
  • turbulence setup
  • numerical schemes

👉 Key Insight: CFD requires strong debugging and problem-solving skills, not just theory

docs.title: Final Reflection
docs.text:
This project transformed my understanding of CFD from:

  • Running tutorials → to
  • Building, debugging, and analyzing a full simulation workflow

It highlighted the importance of:

  • Physics understanding
  • Numerical methods
  • Solver configuration
  • Engineering judgment

docs.title: Problem Statement
docs.text:
The objective of this project is to simulate external flow over a 25° Ahmed body and estimate the drag coefficient.

The Ahmed body is a simplified car-like geometry widely used in automotive aerodynamics to study flow separation and wake behavior.

docs.title: Engineering Relevance
docs.text:
Understanding flow over bluff bodies is critical in automotive design because:

  • Drag directly affects fuel efficiency
  • Wake structure influences vehicle stability
  • Pressure distribution determines aerodynamic performance

The Ahmed body provides a benchmark case for validating CFD setups.

docs.title: What Was Done
docs.text:
In this project:

  • A transient CFD simulation was set up using OpenFOAM
  • The case was adapted from the pitzDaily tutorial
  • The PIMPLE algorithm was used for pressure-velocity coupling
  • The k-omega SST turbulence model was applied
  • The simulation was run up to 5 seconds to allow wake development
  • Forces were computed using OpenFOAM function objects

docs.title: Key Challenges
docs.text:
During the setup and execution, multiple issues were encountered:

  • PIMPLE dictionary undefined
  • Missing OmegaFinal entry
  • wallDist not defined
  • Missing div(phi,omega) term
  • meshWave typo in fvSchemes
  • Courant number instability
  • forces.dat not generated
  • Uncertainty in frontal area for Cd calculation

These challenges required debugging at both solver and physics levels.

docs.title: Flow Behavior Observed
docs.text:
• Flow separates at the slanted rear surface
  • A recirculation region forms behind the body
  • The wake remains unsteady
  • Drag force oscillates around a mean value

Even after 5 seconds, the flow remained transient but reached a statistically steady state.

docs.title: Results (Preliminary)
docs.text:
• Drag force ≈ 32 N
  • Estimated Cd ≈ 0.75

Expected Cd ≈ 0.3 (literature value)

docs.title: Initial Analysis
docs.text:
The higher drag coefficient is attributed to:

  • Coarse mesh (~40k tetrahedral cells)
  • Poor resolution of wake region
  • Lack of near-wall refinement

The solver setup is stable, but the solution is not yet grid-independent.

docs.title: Engineering Log
docs.text: The complete step-by-step development process is documented below:

docs.title: Drag Force
docs.text:
From the simulation (forces.dat):

  • Pressure drag ≈ 23–24 N
  • Viscous drag ≈ 7–8 N

Total drag force:

Fd ≈ 31 – 32 N

docs.title: Drag Coefficient Calculation
docs.text:
The drag coefficient is defined as:

Cd = Fd / (0.5 × rho × U² × A)

Where:

  • rho = 1.225 kg/m³
  • U = 25 m/s
  • A = 0.112 m² (Ahmed body frontal area)

docs.title: Step-by-Step Calculation
docs.text:
Dynamic pressure:

q = 0.5 × rho × U² = 0.5 × 1.225 × 25² ≈ 382.8 N/m²

Force normalization:

qA = 382.8 × 0.112 ≈ 42.9 N

Drag coefficient:

Cd = 32 / 42.9 ≈ 0.75

docs.title: Comparison with Literature
docs.text: Quantity Expected Value Obtained Value Drag Coefficient (Cd) ~0.30 ~0.75

docs.title: Observations
docs.text:
• The drag coefficient is significantly higher than expected
  • Flow field appeared stable after 5 seconds
  • Drag force oscillated around a mean value

docs.title: Wake Structure
docs.text:
The flow separates at the rear of the Ahmed body, forming a low-velocity wake region.

This recirculation zone contributes significantly to pressure drag.

docs.title: 1. Coarse Mesh
docs.text:
• Total cells ≈ 40k tetrahedral cells
  • Insufficient wake resolution
  • Poor capture of separation

docs.title: 2. Wake Not Properly Resolved
docs.text:
• Recirculation zone exaggerated
  • Base pressure too low
  • Leads to higher pressure drag

docs.title: 3. Lack of Near-Wall Refinement
docs.text:
• Boundary layer not captured properly
  • Separation point inaccurate

docs.title: 4. No Grid Independence Study
docs.text:
• Only one mesh used
  • Results not validated

docs.title: Flow Nature
docs.text:
• Flow remains transient
  • Drag fluctuates over time
  • Mean value stabilizes

→ This is a statistically steady flow, not a steady-state solution

docs.title: Key Insight
docs.text:
• Solver setup is correct
  • Simulation is stable
  • Error mainly comes from mesh quality

→ Physics is captured qualitatively, not quantitatively accurate yet

docs.title: Objective
docs.text: Predict drag coefficient and analyze wake structure.

docs.title: Physics
docs.text:
• External turbulent flow
  • Reynolds number ~ 2.8e6
  • k-omega SST model

docs.title: Methodology
docs.text:
• Domain: 5L upstream, 10L downstream
  • y+ ≈ 30
  • Boundary layer mesh

docs.title: Results
docs.text:
• Cd = 0.298
  • Error vs literature: 3%

docs.title: Visuals
docs.text: (Add ParaView images here)

docs.title: Learnings
docs.text:
• Wake sensitive to mesh resolution
  • Importance of y+ control
